response:
## Decision

**Run the full pre-registered grid (Option A)** — 4 arms × 8 tasks × 3 reps, with the pre-reg's own sequential-escalation clause (line 49) as the only mechanism for adding reps/tasks. Reject piloting the Tiered arm or the Opus arm alone with an early stop.

## Rationale

The two probes independently converge on the same conclusion via different routes:

The assumption probe shows the pilot options are self-defeating by construction: the decision rule is a *relative* comparison (Tiered vs. Sonnet-solo, line 51), so a Tiered-only or Opus-only pilot can only decide the gate if that arm lands at an extreme. But the pre-screen (lines 42–43) exists specifically to select the 8 tasks for a Haiku↔Opus quality gradient — i.e., tasks engineered to put the comparison in the ambiguous middle, not at an extreme. The scenario piloting needs (an extreme result) is the scenario the task-selection method is built to exclude. Both pilot options therefore collapse back into Option A almost by design, making the "savings" they promise illusory in the case that matters.

The premortem probe shows why forcing that collapse to happen mid-run (after already declaring the gate "decidable" and stopping) is worse than not attempting the pilot at all: an early "decidable" call would rest on the noisy 1-rep pre-screen plus a partial pilot, not the eval-clustered 95% CI the rule specifies (finding 1); it risks locking in a Tiered result while leaving the Sonnet-solo denominator thin, making the 0.7x-tokens claim unauditable later (finding 2); and it removes exactly the statistical power needed to catch a rubber-stamping validator, which is the specific failure mode this test was pre-registered to catch (finding 3). All three compound the same root problem the assumption probe identifies: a pilot's "decidable" read is least trustworthy on precisely the tasks this method selects for.

## Assumptions and risks (per the required format)

- **Load-bearing assumption:** the owner accepts the ~250–300 invocation spend (line 71) *before* any arm's result is known. If this is false — if the owner is not willing to commit that spend upfront — this decision reverses to a staged/pilot approach and the collapse argument above becomes moot (spend discipline would then dominate over statistical soundness).
- **Failure class accepted:** committing full-grid spend in cases where, in hindsight, one arm would have resolved the gate early and the rest of the spend was unnecessary. This is accepted deliberately: the assumption probe shows we cannot distinguish that case in advance (the pre-screen actively selects against it), and the premortem probe shows the alternative failure class — declaring false decidability off under-powered data — is both more likely and more expensive to discover (potentially only after the config has already shipped and produced unnoticed bad worker output, per finding 3).

## Note on scope

This decision covers only the full-grid-vs-pilot execution-design call. The three "owner calls before spend" in Comment 3 (grader-independence residual, hermetic-control carryover, retry-cap/feedback format) remain separate judgment calls, undecided here.
